title: Day 04 Interview Prep
description: Practice explaining useEffect, dependency arrays, async data fetching, and API UI state machines.

questions.question: What is useEffect and when do you use it?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Side effect definition — not just syntax.
keyPoints: Runs after render commits to screen
Not for transforming data for render — that stays in render
questions.sampleAnswer: useEffect runs code after React paints the UI. I use it when the component needs to sync with something outside React — fetching data, subscribing to events, or updating document title. I do not use it to compute derived data that could be calculated during render.

questions.question: What is the dependency array in useEffect?
keyPoints: [a, b] = when a or b change
questions.sampleAnswer: The dependency array tells React when to re-run the effect. Empty array runs once after mount. Including username re-runs when username changes. Omitting the array runs after every render — dangerous if the effect sets state.

questions.question: How do you fetch data in a React component?
keyPoints: fetch in useEffect or event handler
questions.sampleAnswer: I store data, loading, and error in useState. On search submit or in useEffect when query changes, I call an async function: set loading, fetch, check response.ok, parse JSON into typed state, catch errors, finally clear loading. I never fetch unconditionally during render.

questions.question: Explain async/await vs Promises with fetch.
questions.sampleAnswer: fetch returns a Promise resolving to Response. async/await lets me write sequential code: const res = await fetch(url); const data = await res.json(). Errors throw or require response.ok checks. Under the hood it is still Promises — await is syntactic sugar.

questions.question: What causes infinite loops with useEffect?
keyPoints: Fix: correct deps or move to event handler
questions.sampleAnswer: An effect that calls setState on every run without a stopping condition loops: render, effect, setState, render. Also unstable deps like inline objects cause repeated runs. I fix by adding proper dependencies, guarding with conditions, or moving fetch to button click instead.

questions.question: How do you type API responses in TypeScript?
questions.sampleAnswer: I define an interface matching documented API fields. After await res.json(), I assign to typed state — optionally as GitHubUser if I trust the API, or validate in production. I mark nullable API fields optional. TypeScript helps development; runtime needs zod or similar for untrusted data.

questions.question: Should search fetch on every keystroke or on submit? Trade-offs?
questions.sampleAnswer: Submit is simpler and fewer API calls — good for Day 04. Keystroke search feels faster but floods the API without debounce and causes race conditions. I would debounce 300ms, abort prior request, show loading per query. GitHub API has rate limits — product choice matters.

questions.question: Component fetches in useEffect but shows flash of previous user when username changes. Diagnose and fix.
keyPoints: Clear user state when query changes
questions.sampleAnswer: Race: slow request for 'a' returns after fast request for 'b'. I clear user and set loading when username changes. I abort previous fetch in effect cleanup. Optionally track requestId and ignore stale responses. I might also key the profile card by username to reset UI.
